title: Online Suffix Queries
description: Maintain count of distinct substrings dynamically

examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize empty set S = {}. Step 2: Generate suffixes: starting at index 0: 'abc', index 1: 'bc', index 2: 'c'. Step 3: For suffix 'abc': generate prefixes: 'a', 'ab', 'abc' → add to S: {'a', 'ab', 'abc'}. Step 4: For suffix 'bc': generate prefixes: 'b', 'bc' → add to S: {'a', 'ab', 'abc', 'b', 'bc'}. Step 5: For suffix 'c': generate prefix: 'c' → add to S: {'a', 'ab', 'abc', 'b', 'bc', 'c'}. Step 6: Return size of S = 6.

Step 1: Initialize empty set S = {}. Step 2: Generate suffixes: starting at index 0: 'aa', index 1: 'a'. Step 3: For suffix 'aa': generate prefixes: 'a', 'aa' → add to S: {'a', 'aa'}. Step 4: For suffix 'a': generate prefix: 'a' → add to S: {'a', 'aa'} (duplicate 'a' not added). Step 5: Return size of S = 2.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force with Suffix Set
approaches.summary: For each query, generate all suffixes of the current string, then generate all substrings from each suffix, and store them in a set to count distinct ones. This is straightforward but inefficient for dynamic updates.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n^3) per query where n is string length, due to nested loops for substring generation
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n^2) to store all possible substrings in the worst case

ladders.title: Full Problem — Online Suffix Queries with Brute Force
ladders.desc: Implement a complete solution that maintains count of distinct substrings dynamically using suffix generation and substring extraction.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the brute force approach for the Online Suffix Queries problem. The intuition is straightforward: for each query, we need to generate all possible substrings of the current string and count the distinct ones. We achieve this by first generating all suffixes of the string, then from each suffix, generating all possible prefixes (which are substrings). By storing these in a set, we automatically eliminate duplicates and can simply return the size of the set. The algorithm works as follows: 1) Initialize an empty set to store distinct substrings. 2) For each starting index i from 0 to n-1 (where n is string length), generate the suffix starting at i. 3) For each suffix, generate all prefixes from length 1 to the full suffix length. 4) Add each generated substring to the set. 5) Return the size of the set. Time complexity is O(n³) per query because we have three nested loops: outer loop for suffixes (O(n)), middle loop for substring lengths (O(n)), and inner string slicing (O(n)). Space complexity is O(n²) in worst case to store all possible substrings. This connects directly to the problem requirements by providing a working solution, though inefficient. Common mistakes include: forgetting to clear the set between queries, incorrect loop bounds leading to missing substrings, and not handling empty strings properly. The real code pattern involves careful loop construction and set operations.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty set S = {}. Step 2: Generate suffixes: starting at index 0: 'abc', index 1: 'bc', index 2: 'c'. Step 3: For suffix 'abc': generate prefixes: 'a', 'ab', 'abc' → add to S: {'a', 'ab', 'abc'}. Step 4: For suffix 'bc': generate prefixes: 'b', 'bc' → add to S: {'a', 'ab', 'abc', 'b', 'bc'}. Step 5: For suffix 'c': generate prefix: 'c' → add to S: {'a', 'ab', 'abc', 'b', 'bc', 'c'}. Step 6: Return size of S = 6.

Step 1: Initialize empty set S = {}. Step 2: Generate suffixes: starting at index 0: 'aa', index 1: 'a'. Step 3: For suffix 'aa': generate prefixes: 'a', 'aa' → add to S: {'a', 'aa'}. Step 4: For suffix 'a': generate prefix: 'a' → add to S: {'a', 'aa'} (duplicate 'a' not added). Step 5: Return size of S = 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate All Substrings from Suffixes
ladders.desc: Implement the core function that takes a string and returns all distinct substrings using suffix-based generation.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach. The intuition is that every substring of a string can be uniquely identified by a starting index and an ending index, or equivalently, by a starting index (suffix) and a length (prefix of that suffix). By generating all suffixes and then all prefixes of each suffix, we cover every possible substring exactly once. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty set to store distinct substrings. 2) For each index i from 0 to n-1, get the suffix starting at i (using slicing). 3) For each suffix, iterate over possible lengths l from 1 to the length of the suffix. 4) Extract the substring of length l from the start of the suffix and add it to the set. 5) Return the set. Time complexity is O(n³) because of triple nesting: O(n) for suffixes, O(n) for lengths, and O(n) for slicing in worst case. Space complexity is O(n²) to store substrings. This feeds into the full problem by being the main computation needed for each query. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in loop ranges, not handling empty strings, and inefficient string copying. The real code pattern involves nested loops with careful bounds and set addition.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize set S = {}. Step 2: For i=0, suffix='ab': lengths 1 to 2: length 1 → 'a' → S={'a'}, length 2 → 'ab' → S={'a','ab'}. Step 3: For i=1, suffix='b': length 1 → 'b' → S={'a','ab','b'}. Step 4: Return S.

Step 1: Initialize set S = {}. Step 2: For i=0, suffix='xx': lengths 1 to 2: length 1 → 'x' → S={'x'}, length 2 → 'xx' → S={'x','xx'}. Step 3: For i=1, suffix='x': length 1 → 'x' → S={'x','xx'} (duplicate). Step 4: Return S.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Generating All Suffixes of a String
ladders.desc: Implement the function that generates all suffixes of a given string.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on generating all suffixes, which is the first step in the substring generation process. The intuition is that a suffix is a substring that starts at some position and goes to the end of the string. By taking each possible starting index from 0 to n-1, we can slice the string to get all suffixes. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty list to store suffixes. 2) For each index i from 0 to n-1, slice the string from i to the end and append to the list. 3) Return the list. Time complexity is O(n²) in total for generating all suffixes because each slice operation takes O(n) time and we do it n times. Space complexity is O(n²) to store all suffixes. This feeds into level 1 by providing the suffixes needed for substring extraction. Common mistakes include: missing the full string as a suffix (starting at index 0), including empty suffix (starting at index n), and inefficient slicing. The real code pattern involves a simple loop with string slicing.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize list L = []. Step 2: For i=0, slice 'code'[0:] → 'code', L=['code']. Step 3: For i=1, slice 'code'[1:] → 'ode', L=['code','ode']. Step 4: For i=2, slice 'code'[2:] → 'de', L=['code','ode','de']. Step 5: For i=3, slice 'code'[3:] → 'e', L=['code','ode','de','e']. Step 6: Return L.
Step 1: Initialize list L = []. Step 2: For i=0, slice 'x'[0:] → 'x', L=['x']. Step 3: Return L.

ladders.title: Building Block — Extracting All Prefixes from a String
ladders.desc: Implement a function that generates all prefixes of a given string.
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential because once we have a suffix, we need to extract all its prefixes to get substrings. The intuition is that a prefix is a substring starting at the beginning of the string with increasing lengths. By iterating over possible lengths from 1 to the full length, we can slice the string to get all prefixes. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty list to store prefixes. 2) For each length l from 1 to n (where n is string length), slice the string from index 0 to l and append to the list. 3) Return the list. Time complexity is O(n²) because each slice takes O(n) and we do it n times. Space complexity is O(n²) to store all prefixes. This feeds into level 2 by being the operation applied to each suffix to get substrings. Common mistakes include: starting length from 0 (giving empty string), going up to n-1 (missing full string), and off-by-one errors. The real code pattern involves a loop with string slicing up to a length.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize list L = []. Step 2: For l=1, slice 'prefix'[0:1] → 'p', L=['p']. Step 3: For l=2, slice 'prefix'[0:2] → 'pr', L=['p','pr']. Step 4: For l=3, slice 'prefix'[0:3] → 'pre', L=['p','pr','pre']. Step 5: For l=4, slice 'prefix'[0:4] → 'pref', L=['p','pr','pre','pref']. Step 6: For l=5, slice 'prefix'[0:5] → 'prefi', L=['p','pr','pre','pref','prefi']. Step 7: For l=6, slice 'prefix'[0:6] → 'prefix', L=['p','pr','pre','pref','prefi','prefix']. Step 8: Return L.
Step 1: Initialize list L = []. Step 2: For l=1, slice 'a'[0:1] → 'a', L=['a']. Step 3: Return L.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Slicing to Get Substring
ladders.desc: Implement a function that extracts a substring given start and end indices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is fundamental because both suffix and prefix generation rely on extracting portions of strings. The intuition is that a substring is a contiguous sequence of characters from the original string, defined by a start index (inclusive) and an end index (exclusive). By using array-like indexing or slicing, we can extract this sequence. The algorithm: 1) Given a string s, start index i, and end index j (with 0 ≤ i ≤ j ≤ n, where n is length). 2) Return the slice s[i:j]. Time complexity is O(j-i) for the slice operation. Space complexity is O(j-i) for the new string. This feeds into level 3 as the core operation for getting prefixes and suffixes. Common mistakes include: incorrect bounds leading to index errors, misunderstanding inclusive/exclusive indices, and not handling empty slices. The real code pattern involves direct slicing with careful index validation.

examples.trace: Step 1: String s = 'algorithm', indices: characters: a(0), l(1), g(2), o(3), r(4), i(5), t(6), h(7), m(8). Step 2: Slice s[2:5] means from index 2 inclusive to 5 exclusive. Step 3: Extract characters at indices 2,3,4: 'g','o','r' → 'gor'. Step 4: Return 'gor'.

Step 1: String s = 'slice', indices: s(0), l(1), i(2), c(3), e(4). Step 2: Slice s[0:3] means from index 0 inclusive to 3 exclusive. Step 3: Extract characters at indices 0,1,2: 's','l','i' → 'sli'. Step 4: Return 'sli'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings as Character Sequences
ladders.desc: Learn basic string operations: accessing characters by index and iterating over strings.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept is essential because strings are the primary data structure in this problem. The intuition is that a string is a sequence of characters, and we can access individual characters using indices starting from 0. By understanding this, we can later perform operations like slicing. The algorithm: 1) Given a string s of length n. 2) To access character at index i, use s[i] (with 0 ≤ i < n). 3) To iterate over all characters, use a loop from i=0 to n-1 and access s[i]. Time complexity for access is O(1), for iteration O(n). Space complexity is O(1) for access, O(n) if storing characters. This feeds into level 4 by providing the basic knowledge needed for slicing. Common mistakes include: indexing out of bounds, confusing 0-based indexing, and not handling empty strings. The real code pattern involves simple indexing and loops.

examples.trace: Step 1: String s = 'cat', length n=3. Step 2: Indices: 0 → 'c', 1 → 'a', 2 → 't'. Step 3: Access s[1] returns character at index 1: 'a'. Step 4: Return 'a'.

Step 1: String s = 'dog', length n=3. Step 2: Initialize list L = []. Step 3: For i=0, access s[0]='d' → append to L: ['d']. Step 4: For i=1, access s[1]='o' → append to L: ['d','o']. Step 5: For i=2, access s[2]='g' → append to L: ['d','o','g']. Step 6: Return L.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Suffix Array with LCP
approaches.summary: Build a suffix array and compute the Longest Common Prefix (LCP) array for the current string. The number of distinct substrings is derived from the total possible substrings minus the sum of LCP values. This efficiently handles static strings but requires rebuilding for updates.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n) for building suffix array and LCP, per update or query
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing suffix array and LCP arrays

ladders.title: Full Problem — Online Suffix Queries with Suffix Array and LCP
ladders.desc: Maintain count of distinct substrings dynamically using suffix array and LCP array
ladders.explanation:
This problem requires maintaining the count of distinct substrings in a string that can be updated dynamically. The suffix array approach provides an efficient solution by leveraging the relationship between distinct substrings and longest common prefixes. The intuition is that total possible substrings minus overlapping prefixes gives distinct substrings. For a string of length n, total substrings are n*(n+1)/2. However, many substrings are duplicates - specifically, for any two suffixes, their common prefixes represent duplicate substrings. The LCP array stores the length of longest common prefix between consecutive suffixes in sorted order. Summing all LCP values gives total duplicate prefixes, so distinct substrings = total substrings - sum(LCP).

Algorithm: 1. Initialize with empty string. 2. For each update (append character): a) Update string, b) Build suffix array using doubling algorithm, c) Compute LCP array using Kasai's algorithm, d) Compute distinct substrings = n*(n+1)/2 - sum(LCP). 3. For each query: return current distinct substring count.

Time complexity: O(n log n) per update for suffix array construction, O(n) for LCP computation. Space: O(n) for suffix array, rank array, LCP array.

Connection to L1: This full solution uses the suffix array construction (L1) as its core component.

Common mistakes: 1. Not rebuilding suffix array after updates - must reconstruct entirely. 2. Off-by-one errors in LCP computation. 3. Forgetting to handle empty string case. 4. Integer overflow with large n.

Real code pattern:
def update(char):
    string.append(char)
    n = len(string)
    suffix_array = build_suffix_array(string)
    lcp_array = build_lcp(string, suffix_array)
    total = n*(n+1)//2
    duplicates = sum(lcp_array)
    distinct = total - duplicates
    return distinct

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize empty string, distinct = 0
Step 2: Update 'a': string = 'a', n=1, total=1, LCP sum=0, distinct=1
Step 3: Update 'b': string='ab', n=2, suffix_array=[0,1], LCP=[0], total=3, distinct=3-0=3
Step 4: Update 'a': string='aba', n=3
Suffix array construction:
Suffixes: 'aba', 'ba', 'a'
Sorted: 'a', 'aba', 'ba' → indices [2,0,1]
LCP computation:
Compare 'a' and 'aba': LCP=1 (both start with 'a')
Compare 'aba' and 'ba': LCP=0
LCP array = [1,0], sum=1
Total substrings = 3*4/2=6
Distinct = 6-1=5
Step 5: Query returns 5

Step 1: Empty string, distinct=0
Step 2: Update 'x': string='x', n=1, total=1, distinct=1
Step 3: Update 'y': string='xy', n=2, suffix_array=[0,1], LCP=[0], total=3, distinct=3
Step 4: Update 'z': string='xyz', n=3
Suffixes: 'xyz', 'yz', 'z'
Sorted: 'xyz', 'yz', 'z' → indices [0,1,2]
LCP: 'xyz' vs 'yz'=0, 'yz' vs 'z'=0
LCP array=[0,0], sum=0
Total=6, distinct=6
Query returns 6

testCases.input: Initialize, update 'a', update 'a', update 'a', update 'a', query
Initialize, update 'x', update 'y', update 'z', update 'x', query
Initialize, for i in range(5): update 'a', query
Initialize, update 'm', update 'i', update 's', update 's', update 'i', update 's', update 's', update 'i', update 'p', update 'p', update 'i', query
Initialize, update 'a', update 'b', update 'a', update 'b', update 'a', query

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Suffix Array Construction
ladders.desc: Build sorted array of all suffixes using doubling algorithm
ladders.explanation:
The suffix array is the core data structure that enables efficient substring counting. It's an array containing starting indices of all suffixes sorted lexicographically. The intuition: by sorting suffixes, we can efficiently compute longest common prefixes between consecutive suffixes. The doubling algorithm works by sorting suffixes based on prefixes of increasing length (1, 2, 4, ... characters).

Algorithm: 1. Initialize rank array for single characters. 2. For k from 1 while k < n: a) Create pairs (current rank[i], rank[i+k]) for each suffix, b) Sort suffixes based on these pairs, c) Update ranks based on sorted order. 3. Return sorted indices.

Time: O(n log n) - log n iterations, each with O(n log n) sorting. Space: O(n) for ranks and temporary arrays.

Connection to L0: This subroutine is called every time the string updates to rebuild the suffix array.

Common mistakes: 1. Not handling the case when i+k exceeds string length. 2. Incorrect rank updating logic. 3. Using inefficient sorting (should use counting sort for O(n) per iteration).

Real code pattern:
def build_suffix_array(s):
    n = len(s)
    suffix = list(range(n))
    rank = [ord(c) for c in s]
    k = 1
    while k < n:
        suffix.sort(key=lambda i: (rank[i], rank[i+k] if i+k < n else -1))
        new_rank = [0]*n
        for i in range(1, n):
            prev, curr = suffix[i-1], suffix[i]
            same = (rank[prev] == rank[curr] and 
                   (rank[prev+k] if prev+k < n else -1) == 
                   (rank[curr+k] if curr+k < n else -1))
            new_rank[curr] = new_rank[prev] + (0 if same else 1)
        rank = new_rank
        if rank[suffix[-1]] == n-1: break
        k *= 2
    return suffix

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=3, suffix=[0,1,2], rank=[97,98,97] (ASCII of 'a','b','a')
Step 2: k=1
Sort suffixes by (rank[i], rank[i+1]):
i=0: (97,98), i=1: (98,97), i=2: (97,-1)
Sorted pairs: (97,-1), (97,98), (98,97)
Corresponding indices: [2,0,1]
Update ranks:
new_rank[2]=0
Compare (2,0): ranks differ? rank[2]=97, rank[0]=97 AND (rank[3]? -1) == (rank[1]=98)? No → new_rank[0]=1
Compare (0,1): rank[0]=97, rank[1]=98 differ → new_rank[1]=2
rank=[1,2,0]
Step 3: k=2
Sort by (rank[i], rank[i+2]):
i=2: (0, -1), i=0: (1, -1), i=1: (2, -1)
Already sorted: [2,0,1]
Update ranks: all different → [0,1,2]
rank[2]=2=n-1? Yes, break
Return [2,0,1]

Step 1: n=3, suffix=[0,1,2], rank=[97,98,99]
Step 2: k=1
Sort by (rank[i], rank[i+1]):
(97,98), (98,99), (99,-1) already sorted [0,1,2]
Update ranks: all different → [0,1,2]
rank[2]=2=n-1? Yes, break
Return [0,1,2]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Distinct Substrings Formula from LCP
ladders.desc: Calculate distinct substrings using total substrings minus LCP sum
ladders.explanation:
This is the mathematical insight that makes the approach efficient. The formula: distinct substrings = n*(n+1)/2 - sum(LCP[i] for i=0 to n-2). Intuition: Every substring can be identified by a starting position and length. Total possible substrings count all such pairs. However, when suffixes share prefixes, those prefixes represent duplicate substrings. For two suffixes starting at i and j with LCP length L, they share L substrings (lengths 1 through L). The LCP array contains LCP between consecutive suffixes in sorted order. Summing these gives total duplicate prefixes across all adjacent suffix pairs.

Why it works: 1. Every distinct substring appears as a prefix of some suffix. 2. If a substring appears multiple times, it's a common prefix of multiple suffixes. 3. By sorting suffixes, we capture all duplicate relationships in adjacent comparisons. 4. Sum of LCP counts each duplicate exactly once.

Algorithm: Given suffix array SA and string length n: 1. Compute LCP array (length n-1). 2. total = n*(n+1)/2. 3. duplicates = sum(LCP). 4. Return total - duplicates.

Time: O(n) for summing. Space: O(1) additional.

Connection to L1: Uses suffix array (from L1) as input.

Common mistakes: 1. Forgetting n-1 in LCP array size. 2. Integer division errors. 3. Not handling empty string (n=0).

Real code pattern:
def distinct_substrings(s, suffix_array):
    n = len(s)
    if n == 0: return 0
    lcp = build_lcp(s, suffix_array)  # length n-1
    total = n*(n+1)//2
    duplicates = sum(lcp)
    return total - duplicates

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=3
Step 2: Compute LCP:
Compare suffix at SA[0]=2 ('a') and SA[1]=0 ('aba'): LCP=1
Compare SA[1]=0 ('aba') and SA[2]=1 ('ba'): LCP=0
LCP array = [1,0]
Step 3: total = 3*4/2 = 6
Step 4: duplicates = 1+0 = 1
Step 5: distinct = 6-1 = 5

Step 1: n=3
Step 2: Compute LCP:
SA[0]=0 ('abc') vs SA[1]=1 ('bc'): LCP=0
SA[1]=1 ('bc') vs SA[2]=2 ('c'): LCP=0
LCP = [0,0]
Step 3: total = 6
Step 4: duplicates = 0
Step 5: distinct = 6

ladders.title: Building Block — LCP Array Construction (Kasai's Algorithm)
ladders.desc: Compute Longest Common Prefix array from suffix array
ladders.explanation:
The LCP array stores the length of longest common prefix between consecutive suffixes in the suffix array. Kasai's algorithm computes this in O(n) time using the inverse suffix array (rank array). Intuition: If we know LCP between suffix i and j, then LCP between suffix i+1 and j+1 is at least LCP(i,j)-1. This allows computing LCP values in linear time by processing suffixes in original order.

Algorithm: 1. Compute inverse array inv where inv[SA[i]] = i. 2. Initialize k=0. 3. For i from 0 to n-1: a) If inv[i] == n-1, set k=0, continue. b) j = SA[inv[i]+1] (next suffix in sorted order). c) While i+k < n and j+k < n and s[i+k] == s[j+k], increment k. d) LCP[inv[i]] = k. e) If k > 0, decrement k by 1.

Time: O(n) - each character comparison decreases k, which starts at 0 and never exceeds n. Space: O(n) for inv and LCP arrays.

Connection to L2: Provides the LCP array needed for the distinct substrings formula.

Common mistakes: 1. Not initializing k properly. 2. Off-by-one in array indices. 3. Not handling the last suffix (inv[i] == n-1).

Real code pattern:
def build_lcp(s, suffix_array):
    n = len(s)
    inv = [0]*n
    for i in range(n):
        inv[suffix_array[i]] = i
    lcp = [0]*(n-1)
    k = 0
    for i in range(n):
        if inv[i] == n-1:
            k = 0
            continue
        j = suffix_array[inv[i]+1]
        while i+k < n and j+k < n and s[i+k] == s[j+k]:
            k += 1
        lcp[inv[i]] = k
        if k > 0:
            k -= 1
    return lcp

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=3, compute inv:
SA[0]=2 → inv[2]=0
SA[1]=0 → inv[0]=1
SA[2]=1 → inv[1]=2
inv = [1,2,0]
Step 2: Initialize k=0
Step 3: Process i=0:
inv[0]=1 != 2, so j=SA[2]=1
Compare s[0]='a' vs s[1]='b': different, k=0
lcp[1]=0
Step 4: i=1:
inv[1]=2 == n-1? Yes, set k=0, continue
Step 5: i=2:
inv[2]=0 != 2, j=SA[1]=0
Compare s[2]='a' vs s[0]='a': match, k=1
Compare s[3]? out of bounds, stop
lcp[0]=1
if k>0: k=0
Result: lcp=[1,0]

Step 1: n=6, compute inv from SA
Step 2: k=0
Step 3: Process i=0 (inv[0]=3): j=SA[4]=4
Compare 'banana'[0]='b' vs 'banana'[4]='n': different, lcp[3]=0
Step 4: i=1 (inv[1]=2): j=SA[3]=0
Compare 'a' vs 'b': different, lcp[2]=0
Step 5: i=2 (inv[2]=5): last suffix, k=0
Step 6: i=3 (inv[3]=1): j=SA[2]=1
Compare 'n' vs 'a': different, lcp[1]=0
Step 7: i=4 (inv[4]=4): j=SA[5]=2
Compare 'na' vs 'na': match 2 chars, lcp[4]=2, k=1
Step 8: i=5 (inv[5]=0): j=SA[1]=3
Compare 'a' vs 'a': match, k=2
Compare 'na' vs 'na': match 2 more, total k=4

lcp[0]=4? Wait, need to check: actually 'a' vs 'ana': LCP=1
Let's trace carefully:
i=5, j=3
s[5]='a' vs s[3]='a': match, k=1
s[6]? out, stop
lcp[0]=1
if k>0: k=0
Final lcp=[1,0,0,0,2]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Lexicographic Comparison of Strings
ladders.desc: Compare two strings character by character to determine order
ladders.explanation:
Lexicographic comparison (dictionary order) is fundamental to sorting suffixes. It determines which of two strings comes first in alphabetical order. Intuition: Compare characters from left to right until a difference is found. The string with the smaller character at the first differing position comes first. If one string is a prefix of the other, the shorter string comes first.

Algorithm: 1. Initialize index i=0. 2. While i < len(a) and i < len(b): a) If a[i] < b[i], return -1 (a < b). b) If a[i] > b[i], return 1 (a > b). c) Increment i. 3. If i == len(a) and i == len(b), return 0 (equal). 4. If i == len(a), return -1 (a is prefix, so smaller). 5. Return 1 (b is prefix, so smaller).

Time: O(min(len(a), len(b))) in worst case. Space: O(1).

Connection to L3: Used in LCP computation to compare prefixes.

Common mistakes: 1. Not handling different lengths correctly. 2. Using string comparison operators without understanding implementation. 3. Off-by-one errors in loop conditions.

Real code pattern:
def compare_strings(a, b):
    i = 0
    while i < len(a) and i < len(b):
        if a[i] < b[i]:
            return -1
        if a[i] > b[i]:
            return 1
        i += 1
    if i == len(a) and i == len(b):
        return 0
    if i == len(a):  # a is prefix of b
        return -1
    return 1  # b is prefix of a

examples.trace:
Step 1: i=0, compare 'a' vs 'b'
'a' (97) < 'b' (98) → return -1
Result: 'apple' comes before 'banana'

Step 1: i=0, 'c' vs 'c': equal
Step 2: i=1, 'a' vs 'a': equal
Step 3: i=2, 't' vs 't': equal
Step 4: i=3: 'cat' has length 3, so i == len('cat')
'cat' is prefix of 'catalog' → return -1
Result: 'cat' comes before 'catalog'

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Substrings and Suffixes
ladders.desc: Understand what substrings and suffixes are in a string
ladders.explanation:
A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within a string. For string S of length n, a substring is defined by starting index i (0 ≤ i < n) and length l (1 ≤ l ≤ n-i). Example: In 'hello', substrings include 'h', 'he', 'hel', 'hell', 'hello', 'e', 'el', 'ell', 'ello', 'l', 'll', 'llo', 'l', 'lo', 'o'. A suffix is a special substring that starts at position i and goes to the end of the string. Formally, suffix starting at i is S[i..n-1]. Example: In 'hello', suffixes are 'hello' (i=0), 'ello' (i=1), 'llo' (i=2), 'lo' (i=3), 'o' (i=4).

Key insights: 1. Every substring is a prefix of some suffix. 2. Number of suffixes = n (one starting at each position). 3. Number of total substrings = n*(n+1)/2 (sum of 1 + 2 + ... + n). 4. Distinct substrings are unique among all possible substrings.

Counting: For 'aba' (n=3): total substrings = 3*4/2 = 6. List them: starting at 0: 'a', 'ab', 'aba'; at 1: 'b', 'ba'; at 2: 'a'. Distinct: 'a', 'ab', 'aba', 'b', 'ba' = 5.

Connection to L4: Understanding suffixes is prerequisite for comparing them lexicographically.

Common mistakes: 1. Confusing substring with subsequence (which doesn't require contiguity). 2. Forgetting empty string is not a substring. 3. Off-by-one in indices.

Real code pattern:
def list_substrings(s):
    n = len(s)
    substrings = []
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i+1, n+1):
            substrings.append(s[i:j])
    return substrings

def list_suffixes(s):
    n = len(s)
    suffixes = []
    for i in range(n):
        suffixes.append(s[i:])
    return suffixes

examples.trace:
String length n=2
Substrings:
i=0: j=1 → 'a', j=2 → 'ab'
i=1: j=2 → 'b'
Total: 3 substrings
Suffixes:
i=0 → 'ab'
i=1 → 'b'
Total: 2 suffixes

n=3
Substrings:
i=0: 'a','ab','abc'
i=1: 'b','bc'
i=2: 'c'
Total: 6 = 3*4/2
Suffixes:
i=0: 'abc'
i=1: 'bc'
i=2: 'c'

approaches.name: Approach 3: Dynamic Suffix Automaton
approaches.summary: Use a suffix automaton (or directed acyclic word graph) that supports online character appends. Maintain the automaton incrementally as characters are added, and track the count of distinct substrings using automaton properties like states and transitions.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n) amortized per character addition for updates, O(1) per query for count retrieval

ladders.title: Full Problem — Online Suffix Queries with Dynamic Suffix Automaton
ladders.desc: Implement a data structure that supports appending characters and querying distinct substring count using a suffix automaton.
ladders.explanation: The suffix automaton (SAM) is a finite automaton that compactly represents all substrings of a string. For online updates, we maintain it incrementally as characters are appended. Each state in the automaton represents a set of end positions of substrings, with transitions for character extensions. The key property is that the number of distinct substrings equals the sum over all states of (length of longest string in state - length of link's longest string). To support dynamic updates, we append characters one by one, updating the automaton structure and maintaining the distinct substring count. Intuition: The automaton's states partition substrings by their end equivalence classes, and the link tree (suffix links) helps manage overlaps. When adding a character c, we create a new state for the extended string, clone states if needed to preserve automaton properties, and update links and transitions. The count updates by adding the contribution of new states. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize with a start state. 2. For each new character c: a. Create a new state cur. b. Set length of cur to length of last + 1. c. Traverse from last via suffix links to find the first state with a transition on c, updating transitions. d. If no such state, set link of cur to start. e. Else, let p be that state, and q be its transition on c. If lengths are consecutive, link cur to q. f. Else, clone q to create a new state clone, update lengths and links, and adjust transitions. g. Update count by adding (len[cur] - len[link[cur]]). 3. For queries, return the current count. Time: O(n) amortized per append (each step traverses links, but total operations are linear). Space: O(n) for states and transitions. Connection to L1: This uses the append_character subroutine. Common mistakes: Forgetting to update transitions during cloning, mishandling link updates, or incorrect count calculation. Real code pattern: class SAM: def __init__(self): self.states = [{'len':0, 'link':-1, 'next':{}}]; self.last = 0; self.count = 0. def append(self, c): create cur; set len; p = last; while p != -1 and c not in states[p]['next']: add transition; p = link. if p == -1: link = 0. else: q = next; if len consecutive: link = q. else: clone; adjust. update count. last = cur.

examples.trace: Start: states=[S0], last=0, count=0. Append 'a': create S1 (len=1), p=0, no transition on 'a', add S0->S1 on 'a', p=-1, link S1 to S0, count+=1-0=1, last=1, count=1. Append 'b': create S2 (len=2), p=1, no 'b' at S1, add S1->S2 on 'b', p=0, no 'b' at S0, add S0->S2 on 'b', p=-1, link S2 to S0, count+=2-0=2, total=3, last=2, count=3. Append 'a': create S3 (len=3), p=2, no 'a' at S2, add S2->S3 on 'a', p=0, has 'a' to S1, q=S1, len[S1]=1, len[p]=0, consecutive? len[q] = len[p]+1? 1 = 0+1 yes, link S3 to S1, count+=3-1=2, total=5, last=3, count=5. Queries: after appends: [1,3,5].

Start: states=[S0], last=0, count=0. Append 'a': as above, count=1. Append 'a': create S2 (len=2), p=1, no 'a' at S1? S1 has transition 'a'? In SAM, states have outgoing transitions; initially S1 has none. Actually, after first 'a', S0 has transition 'a' to S1. For second 'a': p=1, check S1: no 'a' transition, add S1->S2 on 'a', p=0, S0 has 'a' to S1, q=S1, len[S1]=1, len[p]=0, consecutive? 1=0+1 yes, link S2 to S1, count+=2-1=1, total=2, last=2. Append 'a': create S3 (len=3), p=2, no 'a' at S2, add S2->S3 on 'a', p=1, no 'a' at S1, add S1->S3 on 'a', p=0, has 'a' to S1, q=S1, len[S1]=1, len[p]=0, consecutive? 1=0+1 yes, link S3 to S1, count+=3-1=2, total=3, last=3. Queries: [1,2,3].

testCases.input: append('a'), query(), append('b'), query(), append('a'), query()
append('a'), append('b'), append('c'), append('a'), append('b'), query()

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Append Character to Suffix Automaton
ladders.desc: Implement the function to add a new character to the automaton and update the distinct substring count.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine handles the incremental update of the suffix automaton when a new character c is appended to the string. The intuition is to extend the automaton to include all new substrings ending with c, leveraging the existing structure to avoid recomputation. The algorithm maintains a last state representing the entire current string. Steps: 1. Create a new state cur with length = length of last + 1. 2. Initialize p to last, and while p is not the start and does not have a transition on c, add a transition from p to cur on c, and move p to its suffix link. This ensures all suffixes that can be extended by c now point to cur. 3. If p becomes -1 (start's link), set cur's link to start. 4. Else, let q be the state reached from p via c. If the length of q is exactly length of p + 1, set cur's link to q (q is the canonical state for the longest suffix). 5. Otherwise, clone q to create a new state clone with length = length of p + 1, copy transitions from q, set q's link to clone, and update transitions from p and ancestors to point to clone instead of q if needed. Then set cur's link to clone. 6. Update the distinct substring count by adding (length of cur - length of cur's link). 7. Set last to cur. Time: O(1) amortized per character, as the while loop traverses suffix links, but each state is processed at most once. Space: O(1) per call, but overall O(n). Connection to L0: This is the core update called for each append in the full problem. Common mistakes: Incorrectly handling the cloning step, not updating all necessary transitions, or miscalculating the count. Real code pattern: def append_char(c): cur = new_state(len=states[last]['len']+1); p = last; while p != -1 and c not in states[p]['next']: states[p]['next'][c] = cur; p = states[p]['link']; if p == -1: states[cur]['link'] = 0; else: q = states[p]['next'][c]; if states[q]['len'] == states[p]['len'] + 1: states[cur]['link'] = q; else: clone = new_state(len=states[p]['len']+1, link=states[q]['link'], next=states[q]['next'].copy()); while p != -1 and states[p]['next'].get(c) == q: states[p]['next'][c] = clone; p = states[p]['link']; states[q]['link'] = clone; states[cur]['link'] = clone; count += states[cur]['len'] - states[states[cur]['link']]['len']; last = cur.

examples.trace: Start: states: S0(len=0,link=-1), S1(len=1,link=0 for 'a'), S2(len=2,link=0 for 'b'), last=2, count=3. Append 'a': cur=S3(len=3), p=2, no 'a' at S2, add S2->S3 on 'a', p=0, S0 has 'a' to S1, q=S1, len[S1]=1, len[p]=0, consecutive? 1=0+1 yes, link S3 to S1, count+=3-1=2, total=5, last=3.

Start: states: S0(len=0,link=-1), S1(len=1,link=0 for first 'a'), S2(len=2,link=S1 for second 'a'), last=2, count=2. Append 'a': cur=S3(len=3), p=2, no 'a' at S2, add S2->S3 on 'a', p=S1, no 'a' at S1, add S1->S3 on 'a', p=0, S0 has 'a' to S1, q=S1, len[S1]=1, len[p]=0, consecutive? 1=0+1 yes, link S3 to S1, count+=3-1=2, total=3, last=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Cloning in Suffix Automaton
ladders.desc: Implement the cloning mechanism to maintain automaton properties when adding characters.
ladders.explanation: Cloning is essential when extending the automaton and encountering a state q whose longest string is not exactly one character longer than p's longest string. This happens because q represents multiple end positions, and we need to split it to preserve the invariant that each state's strings have consecutive lengths. Intuition: The automaton must ensure that for any state, the set of lengths of strings it represents is a contiguous interval. When we try to link a new state cur to q, but q's length is too large, we create a clone of q with adjusted length to serve as the correct link. Steps: 1. Create a new state clone with length = length of p + 1. 2. Copy the transitions and suffix link from q to clone. 3. Update the suffix link of q to point to clone. 4. Traverse back from p via suffix links, and for each state that has a transition on c to q, redirect that transition to clone. This ensures clone inherits the correct transitions. 5. Set the link of cur to clone. Why it works: Clone now represents the suffixes that are exactly one character longer than p's longest string, maintaining the automaton's properties. Time: O(k) where k is the number of states updated, but amortized O(1) per character. Space: O(1) for the new state. Connection to L1: This is a critical part of the append_character subroutine. Common mistakes: Forgetting to copy transitions, not updating all ancestor transitions, or incorrect length assignment. Real code pattern: clone = new_state(len=states[p]['len']+1, link=states[q]['link'], next=states[q]['next'].copy()); states[q]['link'] = clone; while p != -1 and states[p]['next'].get(c) == q: states[p]['next'][c] = clone; p = states[p]['link'];

examples.output: Clone S1 to new state, update links
examples.trace: Scenario: After 'ab', states: S0(len=0), S1(len=1 for 'a'), S2(len=2 for 'b'), last=2. To append 'b': cur=S3(len=3), p=2, no 'b' at S2, add transition, p=0, S0 has 'b' to S2, q=S2, len[S2]=2, len[p]=0, not consecutive (2 != 0+1), so clone q: create clone S4(len=1), copy transitions from S2 (none initially), link of S2 set to S4, update p=0: transition 'b' from S0 was to S2, now to S4, p=-1, link cur to S4, count update etc.

examples.output: No cloning needed as lengths consecutive
examples.trace: After 'aa', states: S0(len=0), S1(len=1 for first 'a'), S2(len=2, link to S1 for second 'a'), last=2. Append 'a': cur=S3(len=3), p=2, no 'a' at S2, add transition, p=S1, no 'a' at S1, add transition, p=0, S0 has 'a' to S1, q=S1, len[S1]=1, len[p]=0, consecutive (1=0+1), so link cur to S1 directly, no cloning.

testCases.input: Clone state with length 3 from q length 5, p length 2
No clone needed for q length 2, p length 1
Clone state with transitions {'a': X, 'b': Y}
Update transitions from p and ancestors
Clone when q length=4, p length=2
Clone when q length=1, p length=0
Clone and update link of q

ladders.title: Building Block — Suffix Links in Automaton
ladders.desc: Understand and implement suffix links that connect states representing suffixes.
ladders.explanation: Suffix links are pointers from each state to another state that represents the longest proper suffix of the strings in the current state. In a suffix automaton, for a state v, its link points to a state w such that the longest string in w is the longest proper suffix of the longest string in v. Intuition: These links form a tree (the suffix link tree) that helps in efficiently traversing suffixes during updates. They ensure that when we add a new character, we can quickly find states that need new transitions. Properties: The start state has link -1. For any state, the length of its link's longest string is strictly less. The links partition the automaton into chains that correspond to suffix chains of the string. Implementation: When creating a new state cur, we set its link based on the traversal from last: if we find a state p with a transition on the new character c to q, and if q's length is p's length + 1, then cur's link is q; otherwise, we may need to clone q. The suffix link tree is used in the append process to propagate transitions. Time: O(1) per state creation. Space: O(n) for storing links. Connection to L2: Suffix links are crucial for determining when to clone states. Common mistakes: Setting links incorrectly, not handling the start state, or misunderstanding the length condition. Real code pattern: Each state has a 'link' field. During append: after traversing p, if p == -1: states[cur]['link'] = 0; else: q = states[p]['next'][c]; if states[q]['len'] == states[p]['len'] + 1: states[cur]['link'] = q; else: proceed to cloning.

examples.output: S0 link -1, S1 link S0, S2 link S0
examples.trace: Start S0: len=0, link=-1. Append 'a': create S1 len=1, p=0, no 'a' transition, add it, p=-1, so link S1 to S0. Append 'b': create S2 len=2, p=S1, no 'b', add S1->S2, p=0, no 'b', add S0->S2, p=-1, link S2 to S0.

examples.output: S0 link -1, S1 link S0, S2 link S1
examples.trace: Start S0: len=0, link=-1. Append 'a': create S1 len=1, p=0, no 'a', add S0->S1, p=-1, link S1 to S0. Append 'a': create S2 len=2, p=S1, no 'a', add S1->S2, p=0, S0 has 'a' to S1, q=S1, len[S1]=1, len[p]=0, consecutive, so link S2 to S1.

testCases.input: State with longest string 'abc', find link
Link when no proper suffix state

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Add Transition in Automaton
ladders.desc: Implement adding a character transition from one state to another in the automaton.
ladders.explanation: A transition in a suffix automaton represents extending a string by a character. Each state has a map from characters to next states. Adding a transition involves updating this map for a given state and character to point to a target state. Intuition: Transitions define how the automaton moves from one state to another when reading characters, capturing all substrings of the string. When appending a new character, we add transitions from states that represent suffixes that can be extended by this character to the new state. This ensures the automaton includes all new substrings. Steps: 1. Check if the state already has a transition on the character; if not, add it. 2. In the context of dynamic updates, we add transitions during the traversal from the last state via suffix links. This operation is O(1) time per addition. Space: O(1) per transition, but overall O(n) in total transitions. Connection to L3: Adding transitions is part of building the automaton using suffix links. Common mistakes: Adding duplicate transitions, not updating the map correctly, or adding transitions from the wrong states. Real code pattern: states[state_id]['next'][char] = target_state_id. In append: while p != -1 and char not in states[p]['next']: states[p]['next'][char] = cur; p = states[p]['link'];

examples.input: Add transition 'a' from state 0 to state 1
examples.trace: Initially, states[0] has next map empty. After operation, states[0]['next'] = {'a': 1}.

examples.input: Add transition 'b' from state 1 to state 2 in automaton for 'ab'
examples.trace: After first 'a', state 1 represents 'a'. When appending 'b', we traverse from last (state 1): p=1, no 'b' transition, so add states[1]['next']['b'] = 2 (new state for 'ab').

testCases.input: Add duplicate transition 'a' from state 0 to state 1
Add multiple transitions from one state
Add transition and then query next state
Add transition for character not in alphabet
Add transition and update during cloning

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — State Representation in Automaton
ladders.desc: Define a state in the suffix automaton with essential properties.
ladders.explanation: A state in a suffix automaton represents a set of end positions in the string that share a common set of substrings. Each state is defined by: 1. length: the length of the longest string in this state. 2. link: the suffix link to another state. 3. next: a dictionary mapping characters to next states (transitions). Intuition: The automaton is a directed graph where states are nodes, and transitions are edges labeled with characters. The length property helps in managing the automaton's structure and calculating the distinct substring count. The link forms a tree that connects states to their suffixes. The next map allows traversal to extended strings. This representation is minimal and captures all substrings efficiently. Implementation: Use a list or array of dictionaries. For example, in Python: states = [{'len': 0, 'link': -1, 'next': {}}] for the start state. New states are appended with appropriate values. Time: O(1) to create a state. Space: O(1) per state. Connection to L4: States are where transitions are added. Common mistakes: Forgetting to initialize all fields, using incorrect data types, or not updating properties when modifying states. Real code pattern: class State: def __init__(self): self.len = 0; self.link = -1; self.next = {}. In practice, use dictionaries in a list for simplicity.

examples.trace: Initialize states list: states = [{'len': 0, 'link': -1, 'next': {}}]. This state represents the empty string.

examples.output: State with len=1, link to start, next may be empty initially
examples.trace: When appending 'a', create new state: states.append({'len': 1, 'link': 0, 'next': {}}). The link is set based on automaton construction.
